channel.title: NYT > Climate and Environment
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Democrats Once Vowed to Stop Oil and Gas. Now They’re Not So Sure.
item.description: As the midterm elections approach, many leading Democrats are rethinking their approach to climate change.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Anna Moneymaker/Getty Images
item.media:description: A talk on rising energy costs on Capitol Hill hosted by Democrats, from left, Sen. Martin Heinrich of New Mexico, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer and Sen. Sheldon Whitehouse of Rhode Island, in March.

item.title: NOAA Issues El Nino Advisory
item.description: The global weather pattern threatens to worsen floods and heat waves already intensifying due to climate change. But it may also mean fewer hurricanes.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.media:credit: Charlie Riedel/Associated Press
item.media:description: Drought-stressed wheat in a field in Kansas last month.

item.title: Indonesia Landslides Devastated Endangered Orangutans, Study Finds
item.description: More than 5 percent of the species is estimated to have been lost when a climate-fueled storm unleashed torrents of water, mud and debris.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn

item.title: Searching for Shade When It’s 125 Degrees
item.description: Every season brings a new struggle for people in Dadu District, Pakistan, an area prone to sandstorms, drought and flooding.
item.dc:creator: Zia ur-Rehman and Asim Hafeez
item.media:credit: Asim Hafeez for The New York Times

item.title: The Secrets of a Soccer-Turf Master
item.description: John Sorochan, a turf scientist at the University of Tennessee, has led the yearslong, multimillion-dollar effort to develop perfect playing fields for the 2026 World Cup.
item.dc:creator: Alan Burdick

item.title: The Scientific Quest for Perfect World Cup Pitch
item.description: Every match must be played on natural grass that gives players as consistent a surface as possible, no matter the venue. Cue the years of sod studies.
item.dc:creator: Alan Burdick

item.title: Judge Blocks National Parks From Removing ‘Negative’ Signs and Depictions of Slavery
item.description: The ruling accused the Trump administration of engaging in censorship by taking down materials at parks across the country.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Hannah Beier for The New York Times
item.media:description: An outdoor exhibit on slavery at Independence National Historical Park in Philadelphia, seen last year, was taken down in January.

item.title: Days of Dangerous Heat Is Expected in the Pacific Northwest
item.description: Portland could climb near 100 degrees this weekend, forecasters said. Little relief is expected until next week.
item.dc:creator: Erin McCann
item.media:credit: Jenny Kane/Associated Press
item.media:description: Many homes in the Pacific Northwest lack air-conditioning.

item.title: Renewable Groups Ask Courts to End Pentagon’s ‘Total Halt’ of Wind Power
item.description: More than 100 planned wind farms in 21 states are now stalled indefinitely as the Pentagon delays military reviews once seen as routine.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Brandon Thibodeaux for The New York Times
item.media:description: The freeze affects 41 wind projects in Texas, which produces more wind power than any other state in the U.S., as well as projects in Colorado, Illinois, Iowa, Kansas, New Mexico, Oregon and Washington.

item.title: The World Is Draining Oil Reserves, Raising Pressure for a Peace Deal
item.description: The amount of oil and fuel stored by businesses and governments has fallen sharply since the start of the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Lazaro Gamio
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: She Used A.I. to Create Better Forecasts for Extreme Weather. Then Her Funding Was Cut.
item.description: Amy McGovern was the director of an A.I. institute dedicated to weather forecasting — until the National Science Foundation ended its funding.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:credit: Brett Deering for The New York Times

item.title: How Should Democrats Talk About Climate Change?
item.description: A big new milestone for solar power, a shift in Democratic messaging and more climate news.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:credit: Tim Gruber for The New York Times
item.media:description: Solar power supplied 12.8 percent of U.S. electricity in May, edging out coal, which supplied just 12.2 percent.

item.title: Scientists Measure Earth’s Vast Underground Fungal Webs
item.description: With machine learning and a high-resolution imaging robot, scientists measured and mapped the extent of Earth’s carbon circulatory system.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes

item.title: Hazardous Temperatures Forecast for Many World Cup Games
item.description: Many of the tournament’s host cities risk hitting dangerous heat levels this year, raising questions about the safety of players and fans.
item.dc:creator: Quinn Glabicki
item.media:credit: Paul Ellis/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Thiago Santos, a player with Brazil’s Fluminense team, during a cooling break at a match in New Jersey last July.

item.title: Millions Across the Midwest and Northeast Face Severe Storms on Thursday
item.description: For the second day in a row, Chicago is in the bull’s-eye of the areas facing severe weather on Thursday, but the storm forecast is widespread.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: In the Fastest-Warming City in the U.S., These Artists See Hope
item.description: At the Nevada Museum of Art, five examples of artist-activists shining a light on the Great Basin Desert and beyond.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Griffin

item.title: Lee Raymond, Who Created Global Oil Behemoth Exxon Mobil, Dies at 87
item.description: He oversaw Exxon’s acquisition of a rival, cut costs relentlessly and denied the scientific consensus on climate change.
item.dc:creator: James R. Hagerty
item.media:credit: Donna McWilliam/Associated Press
item.media:description: Lee Raymond, the chairman and chief executive of Exxon Mobil Corp., at a news conference in 2005. A former high school debating champion, he was known for making withering remarks to those who challenged him.

item.title: Heat in the Northeast Thursday and Friday Brings the Threat of Dangerous Storms
item.description: Soupy, hot weather will settle into the Northeast on Thursday and Friday, bringing with it the threat of dangerous storms.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: A security guard in New York cooling down during a hot day last summer. This week will bring the year’s first widespread dose of summerlike heat and humidity to the city.

item.title: G.M. Plans to Develop Energy Storage Batteries as E.V. Sales Flag
item.description: The carmaker will follow Tesla in making components for large batteries used by electric utilities, data centers and other businesses to handle fluctuations in power supply and demand.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:credit: Rebecca Cook/Reuters
item.media:description: The main active ingredient in the storage batteries that General Motors plans to produce is sodium, which is cheaper and easier to process than lithium.

item.title: The Movement to Stop Data Centers
item.description: What to know about the local backlash against the booming data center industry, the era of me-first energy and more climate news.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.media:credit: Will Warasila for The New York Times
item.media:description: Opposition to a data center in April in Broadview, Mont.

item.title: The Iran War is Forcing Energy-Importing Countries to Turn Inward
item.description: The Iran war is pushing countries to prioritize domestic energy in order to protect themselves from volatile oil and natural gas markets.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jes Aznar for The New York Times
item.media:description: The president of Solaric, a rooftop-solar installer based near Manila, said people were now seeing the technology as “a way to survive this new age.”

item.title: Laboring Under Delhi’s Harsh Heat, Workers Must Choose Health or Wages
item.description: Severe heat waves have been hitting India since April, forcing many of the country’s essential workers to make tough decisions.
item.dc:creator: Pragati K.B., Anupreeta Das and Anindito Mukherjee
item.media:description: Vendors setting up large umbrellas to shade their kiosks last month at a flea market near Jama Masjid, a famous mosque in New Delhi.

item.title: ANWR Oil Draws Few Bids, Despite Trump’s Push for ‘Liquid Gold’
item.description: An auction to drill in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge ended with just 10 percent of the available land claimed for oil development.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Christopher Miller for The New York Times
item.media:description: Caribou on the tundra of the 1002 area of the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge.

item.title: U.S. Forest Service to Open Millions of Acres to Off-Road Vehicles
item.description: The forthcoming order echoes President Trump’s decision to repeal executive orders that have protected other public lands from vehicles for decades.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: The memo directs the U.S. Forest Service to identify which closed “roads, trails, areas, airstrips and waterways” in each national forest might be considered for year-round recreation access.

item.title: Trump Announces $700 Million in Funds Meant to Boost Coal Industry
item.description: The president announced a total of $700 million in federal money to reinvigorate the domestic coal industry, which has been in decline for decades.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Evelyn Hockstein/Reuters
item.media:description: Of the roughly $700 million in total funding, $425 million would be used to upgrade and extend the life of 13 coal plants that otherwise might have closed in the coming years.

item.title: How One Company Plans to Mine the Bottom of the Sea
item.description: A look at the quest to mine the bottom of the ocean for valuable minerals, the connection between light pollution and allergies and more climate news.
item.dc:creator: Harry Stevens
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Urban Light Pollution Might Be Worsening Allergies
item.description: Light pollution prompts plants to shed pollen longer and stronger, according to new research.
item.dc:creator: Marta Zaraska
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times
item.media:description: A study found that trees in cities with brighter lights start producing pollen earlier in the spring and finish later in the fall, adding up to 130 days per year to the allergy season.

item.title: Arizona, Nevada Agree to Trade for Desalinated Pacific Ocean Water
item.description: A decade-old treatment plant in San Diego County, Calif., could leave more water in the Colorado River for states facing severe shortages.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Sandy Huffaker for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left, Dan Denham of the San Diego County Water Authority; Scott Cameron of the U.S. Bureau of Reclamation; Tom Buschatzke of the Arizona Department of Water Resources; and Colby Pellegrino of the Southern Nevada Water Authority toasted the agreement at the Carlsbad Desalination Plant in California on Wednesday.

item.title: E.U. Steps Up Ocean Monitoring as Trump Administration Backs Away
item.description: Days after the U.S. said it would kill a network of ocean monitors, European officials pledged to invest more in their version, calling it a “necessity.”
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.media:credit: Marta Masdeu Navarro/Shutterstock
item.media:description: The European plan to invest roughly $107 million comes as the U.S. has said it would dismantle a sprawling network of sensors.

item.title: Unusual Greenpeace Lawsuit May Proceed, Dutch Court Says
item.description: Greenpeace International is arguing under Dutch law that an American pipeline company, Energy Transfer, sought to silence it with a costly lawsuit in North Dakota.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Alyssa Schukar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Demonstrators against the Dakota Access Pipeline in North Dakota in 2016. The lawsuits are centered on Greenpeace’s involvement in the protests.

item.title: Democrats Pledge to Fight Trump’s Termination of Ocean Monitoring Observatories
item.description: The Trump administration is dismantling a $368 million deep-ocean observation system that monitors marine ecosystems and the effects of climate change.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: National Science Foundation Ocean Observatories Initiative
item.media:description: Recovering a surface buoy — part of a network of moorings, buoys and gliders making observations of the ocean — from the Irminger Sea off Greenland’s coast, in 2021.

item.title: The Iran War Has Led to Months of Global Shortages
item.description: Three months after the Strait of Hormuz was first closed, many countries are experiencing shortages of key goods.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Caitlin Kelly/Associated Press
item.media:description: A farmer throwing organic compost on his fields in Rufisque, Senegal, last month. Some farmers are giving up chemical fertilizers in favor of natural alternatives.

item.title: New York Sues Trump Administration Over TotalEnergies Wind Farm
item.description: The lawsuit argues that it is illegal to pay the French energy giant TotalEnergies $795 million to cancel a planned wind farm off New York.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Joshua A. Bickel/Associated Press
item.media:description: Turbines are visible at Sunrise Wind offshore wind farm under construction off the coast of Montauk Point, N.Y.

item.title: Trump Administration to Dismantle the Ocean Observatories Initiative
item.description: The $368 million network of instruments collecting data in both the Atlantic and Pacific has been critical to climate and ocean research.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:credit: Rebecca Travis/WHOI
item.media:description: A mooring that was used in the Ocean Observatories Initiative was recovered after operating for a year in the Gulf of Alaska.

item.title: I.R.A. Rebates for Appliance Swaps Could Be Phased Out
item.description: New guidance from the Energy Department would prevent people from receiving rebates after making such swaps.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Bing Guan for The New York Times
item.media:description: The new guidance would also end rebates for the replacement of gas-powered dryers, heat pumps and water heaters.
